comments.text: A list of extremizers up to N=69, and some comment on their structure, is given at https://github.com/teorth/erdosproblems/issues/143

Call such a set Sidon with at most one exception (very original name, I know), and let $F(N)$ be the largest size of such a set within $\{1,...,N\}$. Of course, Erdős and Freud have shown that $(\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} + o(1))N^{1/2} \leq F(N)$. I have a nice argument which shows that $F(N) \leq (\sqrt{2} + o(1))N^{1/2}$. If $A$ is Sidon with at most one exception, and $n$ is the unique number that can be represented as a sum in more than one way, then the sets $A \cap [1, n/2]$ and $A \cap (n/2, n]$ are individually Sidon. That is, if $A$ is Sidon with at most one exception, then $A$ can be split into two intervals on which it is Sidon. If we let $h(N)$ be the size of the largest Sidon set in $\{1,...,N\}$, then we therefore have \begin{equation*} F(N) \leq \max_k h(k) + h(N - k). \end{equation*} It is known (see [30]) that $h(N) = N^{1/2} + o(N^{1/2})$. Using calculus, you can see that for fixed $N$, the value $k^{1/2} + (N - k)^{1/2}$ is never greater than $\sqrt{2}(N^{1/2})$. It follows that $F(N) \leq (\sqrt{2} + o(1))N^{1/2}$.
